courses.name: Writing and Language I
courses.description: The first half of an introduction to the principles, styles, and structures of effective writing, including essay writing. Course offered only in Nunavut as part of Certificate in Nunavut Public Service Studies Program.

courses.name: Writing and Language II
courses.description: The second half of an introduction to the principles, styles, and structures of effective writing, including essay writing. Course offered only in Nunavut as part of Certificate in Nunavut Public Service Studies Program.

courses.name: English Grammar: Fundamentals
courses.description: A practical and intensive overview of English grammar designed for students who want to improve their understanding of grammar for their own writing and reading. This is not an ESL course.

courses.name: Literature in Global Context
courses.description: Introduction to the study of literature from a global perspective. Students will be exposed to writers from various locations and to methods for studying literature across national boundaries.

courses.name: Writing Essays about Literature
courses.description: An intensive writing course focusing on the formulation and construction of a literary essay.

courses.name: Effective Writing
courses.description: The rhetorical principles, skills, and structures necessary for the kind of writing done at the university level. Clear and effective composition as a mode of research, discovery, analysis, and persuasion. Students pursuing the English major or minor should take instead of .

courses.name: Literature, Law, and Criminality
courses.description: An introductory course whose readings focus on the intersections between literature, law, and criminality.  Topics will vary.  Consult the English Department website for the current topic.

courses.name: Literature, Science, and Technology
courses.description: An introductory course whose readings focus on the intersections between literature, science, and technology.  Topics will vary.  Consult the English Department website for the current topic.

courses.name: Literature, Psychology, and the Mind
courses.description: An introductory course whose readings focus on the intersections between literature, psychology, and the mind.  Topics will vary.  Consult the English Department website for the current topic.

courses.name: Literature, Art, and Culture
courses.description: An introductory course whose readings focus on the intersections between literature, art, and culture. Topics will vary. Consult the English Department website for the current topic.

courses.name: Introduction to Creative Writing
courses.description: An introduction to the practice of creative writing, focusing on poetry, the short story, creative non-fiction, and drama. Emphasis is also placed on contextualizing creative writing as an academic discipline, a mode of self-expression, and a professional industry.

courses.name: Literature and Magic
courses.description: An introductory course whose readings focus on the intersections between literature and magic.  Topics will vary.  Consult the English Department website for the current topic.

courses.name: Introduction to Drama Studies
courses.description: An introduction to drama studies, combining attention to theatre history, conventions, and devices, with attention to theatrical practice, and interpretation of selected dramatic texts. Students will develop a vocabulary for speaking and writing with confidence about theatrical productions, theatre practice, and dramatic texts.

courses.name: Climate Change and the Humanities
courses.description: An introduction to literature and culture in the context of the environmental humanities and climate change.

courses.name: Theory and Criticism
courses.description: An introduction to theories and methods of literary analysis. Through the study of literature, theory, and criticism, students will explore disciplinary history, critical terms, textual analysis, and research methods.

courses.name: Myth and Symbol
courses.description: A literary study of myths and symbols from oral traditions to contemporary forms through selected interdisciplinary and  theoretical approaches.

courses.name: Children's Literature
courses.description: An introduction of the critical study of children’s literature.

courses.name: Greek and Roman Epic
courses.description: An examination of the genre of epic in Greco-Roman antiquity, including a close reading of translations of Homer and Vergil.

courses.name: Topics in Popular Culture
courses.description: Study of a selected topic related to popular culture.

courses.name: Introduction to the Novel
courses.description: A historical and critical study of the novel.

courses.name: Drama Workshop
courses.description: A course dealing with the rudiments of theatrical performance: voice, movement, improvisation, interpretation. Exercises are based upon examples drawn from classical and contemporary repertoires.

courses.name: History of the English Language
courses.description: A historical study of the English language, its structure, variety, and cultural contexts, with an introduction to grammatical terminology and constructions.

courses.name: Topics in Popular Fiction
courses.description: An introduction to the critical study of popular fiction. Topics will vary but may include popular narrative forms such as fantasy, horror, mystery, romance, Young Adult (YA) fiction, etc.

courses.name: Science Fiction
courses.description: A study of the history and traditions of science fiction, speculative fiction, fantasy, and utopia, covering various periods, nationalities, genres, and/or media.

courses.name: Women and Literature
courses.description: Representations of women and the construction of femininity in selected literary texts, the position of women as readers and authors, and the impact of feminist criticism on literary analysis.

courses.name: Gender, Sexuality and Literature
courses.description: How literature represents, reproduces, and resists cultural notions of gender and sexuality. Topics may include: gender and sexuality in relation to literary history, production, and reception; literature by/about “deviant” or subcultural sexualities and genders.

courses.name: Creativity, Imagination, and Writing
courses.description: This course not only surveys theories about the imagination and creativity but also teaches various rhetorical exercises and strategies for sparking inventive thinking and new ideas to fire the writing process.  Consult the English Department's website for detailed information.

courses.name: The Pleasures of Reading
courses.description: This course introduces majors and non-majors to a selection of known and unknown "masterpieces."  Texts may be grouped to explore specific themes.  Requirements include a variety of assignments but no formal essay.  Consult the English Department's website for detailed information.

courses.name: Weird Fiction
courses.description: Introduction to a sub-category of speculative fiction that spans from traditional ghost stories and tales of the macabre to the “New Weird”: contemporary writing that overthrows the clichés, conventions, and expectations of fantasy, horror, and science fiction.

courses.name: Literatures and Cultures 500-1500
courses.description: A study of the period between 500 and 1500, with attention to cultural, historical, geographical, and literary contexts.

courses.name: Literatures and Cultures 1500-1700
courses.description: A study of the period between 1500 and 1700, with attention to cultural, historical, geographical, and literary contexts.

courses.name: Introduction to Digital Humanities
courses.description: An introduction to the principal debates in and approaches to the Digital Humanities.

courses.name: Digital Humanities:  Theory and Method
courses.description: A multidisciplinary survey of core theories, methodologies and tools within the Digital Humanities. Assignments will include collaborative work and applied projects.

courses.name: Classical Mythology
courses.description: A study of classical mythology, emphasizing its use in Greek and Roman literature and its place in classical art and religion. There is some discussion of classical myths in terms of contemporary interpretations of myth.

courses.name: History of World Cinema I
courses.description: Historical survey of world cinema primarily from 1895 to 1945, examining the forms, structures and stylistic conventions of various periods and nations.

courses.name: History of World Cinema II
courses.description: Historical survey of world cinema primarily since 1945, examining the forms, structures and stylistic conventions of various periods and nations.

courses.name: Greek and Roman Drama
courses.description: An examination of the genres of tragedy and comedy in Greco-Roman antiquity.

courses.name: Drama: Modes and Movements
courses.description: A study of dramatic texts and traditions, offering attention to major dramatic modes and movements such as Ritual, Dance, Naturalism, Expressionism, Absurdism, Political Theatre, Feminist Theatre, and Global/Intercultural Theatre. Each will be investigated in the context of performance videos, live performances, and/or written text.

courses.name: American Literatures I
courses.description: Introduction to the traditions of American literature through 1865.

courses.name: American Literatures II
courses.description: Introduction to the traditions of American literature after 1865.

courses.name: Indigenous Drama
courses.description: A study of dramatic literatures and theatre practice from Indigenous theatre makers, including playwrights, directors, and other practitioners.

courses.name: Culture and Climate Change
courses.description: Selected topics related to climate change and cultural studies.

courses.name: Indigenous and Canadian Literatures
courses.description: A survey of  Canadian literary cultures in English from their beginnings to the present that frames them in the wider context of Indigenous writing and storytelling. This course is writing-attentive.

courses.name: Literature of the Self
courses.description: A study of developments in the literary representation of the self. The course considers a wide range of major texts from the Middle Ages to the present.

courses.name: Writing Poetry
courses.description: A workshop involving regular assignments in writing poetry and practical criticism based on this work. Permission to register in this course  requires the student to submit a writing sample. Instructions can be found at carleton.ca/english.

courses.name: Writing Fiction
courses.description: A workshop involving regular assignments in writing prose fiction and practical criticism based on this work. Permission to register in this course  requires the student to submit a writing sample. Instructions can be found at carleton.ca/english.

courses.name: Culture and Society
courses.description: A study of literature in relation to its social and political contexts. Topics and periods vary.

courses.name: Celtic Literatures
courses.description: The literatures of Ireland, Scotland, and/or Wales. Topics will vary in national and historical scope and may be organized by theme, author, and/or genre.

courses.name: Book Arts Workshop
courses.description: This experiential learning course immerses students in the practical arts and histories of book production.

courses.name: Writing Creative Nonfiction
courses.description: A workshop involving regular assignments in reading and writing creative nonfiction and practical criticism based on this work.  Permission to register in this course requires the student to submit a writing sample.  Instructions can be found at carleton.ca/english.

courses.name: Topics in Decolonization and Migration I
courses.description: An introduction to the study of literature and culture in the context of topics such as empire and decolonization, diaspora, migration and globalization, race, and ethnicity. Themes, authors, and geographical and temporal focus will vary.

courses.name: African Literatures I
courses.description: An introductory survey of modern African literatures, discourses, and cultural production in the first half of the 20th century.

courses.name: African Literatures II
courses.description: A survey of modern African literatures, discourses, and cultural production from the era of political independence from colonialism (the 1960s) to the present.

courses.name: South Asian Literatures I
courses.description: An introductory historical survey of the literatures of South Asia to the early colonial era, starting with the Indian epics and concluding with literary traditions of 18th-century India.

courses.name: South Asian Literatures II
courses.description: An introductory survey of literatures of South Asia from the colonial and postcolonial eras.  Topics include the nationalist movement, neo-colonialism, and post-colonialism.

courses.name: Literatures of the Americas I
courses.description: Introduction to comparative and transnational approaches to the literatures and oratures of the Caribbean, and North and South America, with emphasis on the pre-colonial and colonial eras.

courses.name: Literatures of the Americas II
courses.description: Introduction to comparative and transnational approaches to 20th- and 21st-century writing from the Caribbean, and North and South America.

courses.name: Literatures in Translation
courses.description: A study of non-English literatures in translation with a special focus on cultural and historical contexts.

courses.name: Reading Poetry
courses.description: This course is designed to enable students to develop skills in reading and writing about poetry. Readings will be chosen from a variety of authors, periods, and/or genres.

courses.name: Studies in Greek Literature
courses.description: A study of an author or topic in Greek literature. Contents of this course vary from year to year.

courses.name: Studies in Roman Literature
courses.description: A study of an author or topic in Roman literature.

courses.name: The Secret Lives of Poems
courses.description: This course is designed to enable students to develop skills in reading and writing about great works of poetry. Course requirements will feature a combination of creative and critical exercises, but no formal essay.

courses.name: Comics and Graphic Novels
courses.description: An introduction to the critical study of comic books and graphic narrative.

courses.name: Europe, Russia, and Eurasia Beyond Borders: Literature and Culture
courses.description: An exploration of the cultural borders and boundaries of contemporary Europe, Russia, and Eurasia. Using literary and visual texts, the course explores issues such as migration, cultural and political borders and their transcendence, cultural responses to authoritarianism, the Cold War and its afterlives, and memory.

courses.name: History of Literary Theory
courses.description: Introduction to ideas about literature, aesthetics, authorship, and readership as these have circulated in periods before the twentieth century.

courses.name: Theories and Critical Practices
courses.description: This course offers students an interdisciplinary foundation in cultural, critical, and literary theories and practices.  This course is writing attentive.

courses.name: Topics in Medieval Literature
courses.description: A study of selected topics and texts from medieval literature.

courses.name: European Literature
courses.description: Major movements and works from Dante's Divine Comedy through Voltaire's Candide. Themes include the New Humanism vs. old Chivalry in the Renaissance and Baroque periods; the rise of the modern novel and drama; reason, nature, and the Enlightenment project.

courses.name: Chaucer
courses.description: A study of Chaucer's works including some attention to the Middle English language in which he wrote.

courses.name: Literary Representations of Childhood and Youth
courses.description: An examination of the ways in which childhood, children, and youth have been represented in creative literature (fiction, poetry, drama, and/or creative nonfiction).

courses.name: Shakespeare and the Stage
courses.description: Introduction to the study of early modern play-texts written by Shakespeare and/or his contemporaries.

courses.name: Shakespeare and Film
courses.description: A study of film adaptations of selected plays by Shakespeare.

courses.name: The Book in the Digital Age
courses.description: A multidisciplinary course focused on the social, economic and political dimensions of the book in its manuscript, print and digital forms.

courses.name: 18th-Century Literature
courses.description: A detailed study of authors and movements of the period 1660 to 1780.

courses.name: Introduction to Professional Writing and Editing
courses.description: The fundamental skills of professional writing and editing, including writing for specific audiences, document design, revision strategies, copyediting.

courses.name: Professional Writing Practicum
courses.description: Experiential learning in Professional Writing via field placement. Students pursue personalized learning outcomes in a workplace practicum. The submission of an application is required.

courses.name: Literatures and Cultures 1700-1900
courses.description: A study of the period between 1700 and 1900, with attention to cultural, historical, geographical, and literary contexts.

courses.name: Literatures and Cultures 1900-Now
courses.description: A study of the period between 1900 and the present, with attention to cultural, historical, geographical, and literary contexts.

courses.name: The 19th-Century Novel
courses.description: A study of the English novel in the 19 th  century.

courses.name: 20th- and 21st-Century Poetry
courses.description: A study of 20th and 21st-century poetry in English. Topics and authors may vary.

courses.name: 20th- and 21st-century Fiction
courses.description: A study of 20th- and 21st-century fiction in English. Topics and authors may vary.

courses.name: Modern and Contemporary Literary Theory
courses.description: Introduction to contemporary approaches to literary texts, such as formalist, structuralist, deconstructive, psychoanalytic, Marxist, historicist, and feminist. Topics may include: the nature and role of literature, of author and reader, of canons, ideology, gender, sexuality, and race.

courses.name: Topics in Theatre Management
courses.description: A workshop taught by practitioners in the community that provides students with the knowledge and skills necessary to create, manage, and sustain theatre projects. Topics will vary but may include the development of children’s theatre or the operation of a festival or touring company.

courses.name: Drama:  Contemporary Performance
courses.description: A study of dramatic texts and performance practices in contemporary professional theatre.  Topics vary according to the season programs of professional theatre in Ottawa.  Students will attend a number of productions, determined by the instructor.  Field trip fees will apply.

courses.name: American Culture
courses.description: A study of American writing in its cultural and historical contexts.

courses.name: Canadian Poetry
courses.description: A study of Canadian poetry in its social and political contexts.

courses.name: Canadian Fiction
courses.description: A study of Canadian fiction in its social and political contexts.

courses.name: Writing Screenplays
courses.description: An intermediate workshop involving regular assignments in writing for film.

courses.name: Writing Fiction (Intermediate)
courses.description: An intermediate workshop involving regular assignments in writing prose fiction and practical criticism.

courses.name: Intermediate Drama Workshop
courses.description: A course dealing with techniques of characterization, principles of ensemble performance, scene analysis for actors and directors, styles of performance.

courses.name: Topics in Performance
courses.description: A study of selected elements of performance. Topics will vary but may include such areas as the theory and practice of comic timing on stage or movement on stage space.

courses.name: Writing Popular Fiction
courses.description: An intermediate workshop in creative writing that focuses on the development of writing skills specific to the crafting of narratives in such genres as Speculative Fiction, Young Adult Fiction, and Historical Fiction.

courses.name: Research and Theory in Academic Writing
courses.description: Study of contemporary research and theory (1970s to present) on academic writing in elementary, secondary and post-secondary school, with emphasis on writing in university. Consideration of what academic writing entails, how writing fosters learning, and how instruction can help students develop their writing abilities.

courses.name: Research and Theory in Workplace Writing
courses.description: Study of contemporary research and theory (1980s to present) in writing in workplace settings. Consideration of how writing is used in accomplishing work, how novices learn to write effectively, and what the implications are for pedagogy.

courses.name: From English Degree to Career
courses.description: This experiential-learning course prepares students in English for their transition into the workplace. Project-based activities (including readings and research) and guest speakers will teach students to identify, develop, and apply the skills and knowledge gained from a degree in English studies.

courses.name: Cultural Studies
courses.description: This course explores cultural expression across diverse media, theorizing culture as a form of struggle that shapes material conditions, fuels knowledge production, and informs lived experience.

courses.name: Special Topics in Writing
courses.description: An intermediate workshop that involves regular creative writing assignments and practical criticism based on this work. Topics will vary. Yearly special topics can be found at carleton.ca/english/.

courses.name: Spoken Word Poetry Workshop
courses.description: This intermediate-level workshop-based course explores traditions of spoken word poetry while requiring students to create and perform their own spoken word poems.

courses.name: Literary Ecological Fieldwork
courses.description: This interdisciplinary, experiential fieldwork course brings together literature, culture, and ecology studies. At least 50% of class periods will be devoted to short field work excursions in the Ottawa region. These excursions will be complemented by classroom discussion time. Field trip fees will apply.

courses.name: Topics in Decolonization and Migration II
courses.description: An intermediate study of literature, culture, and research in the context of topics such as empire and decolonization, diaspora, migration and globalization, race, and ethnicity. Themes, authors, and geographical and temporal focus will vary.

courses.name: Studies in Diaspora Lit.
courses.description: A study of diaspora literatures and cultures.

courses.name: Studies in Indigenous Literature
courses.description: A study of Indigenous literatures and cultures.

courses.name: Intro to Postcolonial Theory
courses.description: A survey of major concepts and key figures in postcolonial theory.

courses.name: Studies in Postcolonial Literature
courses.description: A study of postcolonial literatures and cultures. Topics may vary from year to year.

courses.name: Studies in Poetry
courses.description: A study of a selected topic in poetry.

courses.name: Studies in the Novel
courses.description: A study of a selected topic in the novel.

courses.name: Writing and Knowledge-Making in the Professions
courses.description: The role of writing in constructing knowledge in the professions, as viewed from contemporary socio-cultural perspectives.  Consideration of how the goals, values, and assumptions of different professions shape their writing in distinctive ways and what implications this holds for theory, research, and practice.

courses.name: Studies in Literary Theory
courses.description: Study of a selected topic in literary theory and criticism.

courses.name: Old English
courses.description: Studies in Old English literature and its cultural and historical contexts. Instruction in grammar to facilitate reading knowledge of the Old English language.

courses.name: Culture and the Text
courses.description: Topics will vary from year to year.

courses.name: Digital Culture and the Text I
courses.description: A study of new developments in digital media and culture, and how they affect our understanding of literary modes, genres and textuality, including notions of authorship and reading strategies.  Topics will vary from year to year.

courses.name: Studies in Publishing
courses.description: Topics will vary from year to year.

courses.name: Editing a Literary Magazine
courses.description: In this experiential learning course students will work collaboratively to design, edit, produce, and publicize issues of a Carleton University literary magazine in digital and/or print formats.

courses.name: Digital Culture and the Text II
courses.description: A study of new developments in digital media and culture, and how they affect our understanding of literary modes, genres and textuality, including notions of authorship and reading strategies.  Topics will vary from year to year.

courses.name: Studies in Digital Humanities
courses.description: A study of current issues and debates in the Digital Humanities.

courses.name: Studies in Medieval Literature
courses.description: A study of a selected topic in Medieval literature; requires previous experience reading medieval English.

courses.name: Studies in Renaissance Literature
courses.description: A study of a selected topic in Renaissance literature.

courses.name: Studies in 18th-Century Literature
courses.description: A study of a selected topic in Restoration or 18th-century literature.

courses.name: Digital Humanities Workshop
courses.description: This workshop will provide students with the opportunity to complete an individual or collaborative capstone project in the Digital Humanities.

courses.name: Digital Humanities Practicum
courses.description: Practical experience gained by working on projects under the supervision of the staff of a participating public- or private-sector institution or organization, including a final written assignment or equivalent project. A maximum of 1.0 practicum credit may be applied towards degree requirements.

courses.name: Studies in Romanticism
courses.description: A study of a selected topic, 1770-1830.

courses.name: Teaching Writing in School and the Workplace
courses.description: Introduction to approaches for teaching writing in elementary and secondary school, in university, and in the workplace, with a focus on socio-cultural theories of language and learning. Discussion of applications of these approaches to classroom and workplace teaching.

courses.name: Studies in Victorian Literature
courses.description: A study of a selected topic in 19th-century British literature, 1830-1900.

courses.name: The Great Russian Novel
courses.description: A study of masterpieces of the Russian tradition, to be selected from among works by writers such as Tolstoy, Dostoevsky, Gogol, Turgenev, Bely, Bulgakov, and Nabokov. All novels will be read in English translation.

courses.name: Studies in Contemporary Poetry
courses.description: A comparative and transnational approach to 20th- and 21st -century poetry.

courses.name: Theatre Production Seminar
courses.description: This course offers students advanced engagement with the theory and application of theatrical crafts and includes participation in a writing, acting, or technical capacity on a class production.

courses.name: Studies in 20th- and 21st-century Literature
courses.description: A study of a selected topic in literature of the 20th and 21st century.

courses.name: Global Stages and Theories
courses.description: An advanced study of dramatic texts from transnational, postcolonial, or European contexts. This course will offer sustained attention to specific theatre traditions, theatrical practice, and interpretation of texts. Topics and points of emphasis vary from year to year.

courses.name: Studies in American Literature I
courses.description: A study of a selected topic in American literature.

courses.name: Studies in American Literature II
courses.description: A study of a selected topic in American literature.

courses.name: Race, Ethnicity and Canadian Lit.
courses.description: A study of Canadian literature that engages with notions of race and ethnicity.

courses.name: Studies in Canadian Literature I
courses.description: A study of a selected topic in Canadian literature.

courses.name: Studies in Canadian Literature II
courses.description: A study of a selected topic in Canadian literature.

courses.name: Independent Study
courses.description: Independent research and writing, under the supervision of English faculty, requiring an essay of approximately 10,000 words. A written proposal outlining the project must be submitted to the undergraduate supervisor by July 31.

courses.name: Writing and Knowledge-Making in the Disciplines
courses.description: The role of writing in constructing knowledge in academic disciplines, as viewed from contemporary socio-cultural perspectives. Consideration of how the goals, values, and assumptions of different disciplines shape their writing in distinctive ways and what implications this holds for pedagogy.

courses.name: Independent Creative Writing Project
courses.description: Independent creative writing, supervised by a Departmental faculty member, resulting in a poetry manuscript (10-15 poems), a one-act play, a 10,000-word novella, or two short stories.  A proposal, coordinated with the faculty supervisor, must be submitted to the Undergraduate Supervisor by July 31.

courses.name: Advanced Writing Workshop
courses.description: An advanced workshop involving regular assignments in creative writing and practical criticism based on this work.  Topics will vary.

courses.name: Issues in Diaspora Literature
courses.description: A study of a selected topic in diaspora literature and culture.

courses.name: Topics in Postcolonial and Diaspora Lit. and Theory
courses.description: A study of a selected topic in postcolonial and/or diaspora literatures and theories. Themes, authors, and geographical and temporal focus will vary.

courses.name: Indigenous Literatures I
courses.description: A study of the literatures produced by Indigenous storytellers and writers, with a focus on the oral tradition and life writing.

courses.name: Indigenous Literatures II
courses.description: A study of the contemporary period of Indigenous literature, examining the historical and mythic influences on the literature.

courses.name: Issues in Postcolonial Theory
courses.description: A study of a selected issue in postcolonial and/or diaspora theory.

courses.name: Issues in Postcolonial Literature
courses.description: A study of a selected topic in postcolonial literature and culture.

free_electives.degree: English with Concentration in Creative Writing B.A. Honours (20.0 credits)
English with Concentration in Drama Studies B.A. Honours (20.0 credits)

free_electives.text: 9.  Sufficient free elective credits to make up 20.0 credits total for the program.

free_electives.degree: Specialization in Global Literatures B. G. In. S. Honours (20.0 credits)
Stream in Global Literatures B.G.In.S. (15.0 credits)
